sources.originalContent:
Information security has always been a complex subject, and it evolves quickly with the creative ideas and implementations of attackers and security researchers.  

Security is one of the most important aspects of any architecture. Good security provides confidentiality, integrity, and availability assurances against deliberate attacks and abuse of your valuable data and systems. Losing these assurances can harm your business operations and revenue, and your organization's reputation.

> [!NOTE]
> Learn how cloud security is an ongoing journey of incremental progress and maturity, in [Security in the Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure](/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/secure). Learn how to build security into your solution, in the Azure Well-Architected Framework [Overview of the security pillar](/azure/architecture/framework/security/overview).

Here are some broad categories to consider when you design a security system: 

![Image that shows categories to consider when you design a security system.](images/security-overview.png) 

Azure provides a wide range of security tools and capabilities. These are just some of the key security services available in Azure:

- [Microsoft Defender for Cloud](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/defender-for-cloud). A unified infrastructure security management system that strengthens the security posture of your datacenters. It also provides advanced threat protection across your hybrid workloads in the cloud and on-premises.
- [Microsoft Entra ID](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/active-directory). The Microsoft cloud-based identity and access management service.  
- [Azure Front Door](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/frontdoor). A global, scalable entry-point that uses the Microsoft global edge network to create fast, highly secure, and widely scalable web applications.
- [Azure Firewall](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/azure-firewall). A cloud-native, intelligent network firewall security service that provides threat protection for your cloud workloads that run in Azure.
- [Azure Key Vault](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/key-vault). A high-security secret store for tokens, passwords, certificates, API keys, and other secrets. You can also use Key Vault to create and control the encryption keys used to encrypt your data.
- [Azure Private Link](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/private-link). A service that enables you to access Azure PaaS services, Azure-hosted services that you own, or partner services over a private endpoint in your virtual network. 
- [Azure Application Gateway](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/application-gateway). An advanced web traffic load balancer that enables you to manage traffic to your web applications. 
- [Azure Policy](https://azure.microsoft.com/services/azure-policy). A service that helps you enforce organizational standards and assess compliance.

For a more comprehensive description of Azure security tools and capabilities, see [End-to-end security in Azure](/azure/security/fundamentals/end-to-end).

## Introduction to security on Azure

If you're new to security on Azure, the best way to learn more is with [Microsoft Learn training](/training/?WT.mc_id=learnaka). This free online platform provides interactive training for Microsoft products and more.

Here are two learning paths to get you started:

- [Microsoft Azure Fundamentals: Describe general security and network security features](/training/paths/az-900-describe-general-security-network-security-features)

- [Microsoft Security, Compliance, and Identity Fundamentals: Describe the capabilities of Microsoft security solutions](/training/paths/describe-capabilities-of-microsoft-security-solutions)

## Path to production

- To secure Azure application workloads, you use protective measures like authentication and encryption in the applications themselves. You can also add security layers to the virtual machine (VM) networks that host the applications. See [Firewall and Application Gateway for virtual networks](../../example-scenario/gateway/firewall-application-gateway.md) for an overview.
- Zero Trust is a proactive, integrated approach to security across all layers of the digital estate. It explicitly and continuously verifies every transaction, asserts least privilege, and relies on intelligence, advanced detection, and real-time response to threats.

   For an implementation strategy for web apps, see [Zero Trust network for web applications with Azure Firewall and Application Gateway](../../example-scenario/gateway/application-gateway-before-azure-firewall.md).

- Azure governance establishes the tooling needed to support cloud governance, compliance auditing, and automated guardrails. See [Azure governance design area guidance](/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/ready/landing-zone/design-area/governance) for information about governing your Azure environment.

## Best practices

The Azure Well-Architected Framework is a set of guiding tenets, based on five pillars, that you can use to improve the quality of your architectures. For more information, see [Overview of the security pillar](/azure/architecture/framework/security/overview) and [Security design principles in Azure](/azure/architecture/framework/security/security-principles).

The Well-Architected Framework also provides these checklists:

- [Azure identity and access management considerations](/azure/architecture/framework/security/design-identity)
- [Network security](/azure/architecture/framework/security/design-network)
- [Data protection considerations](/azure/architecture/framework/security/design-storage) 
- [Governance, risk, and compliance](/azure/architecture/framework/security/design-governance)

## Security architectures
 
### Identity and access management

- [Microsoft Entra identity management and access management for AWS](../../reference-architectures/aws/aws-azure-ad-security.yml)

### Threat protection

- [Multilayered protection for Azure virtual machine access](../../solution-ideas/articles/multilayered-protection-azure-vm.yml)

## Stay current with security

Get the latest updates on [Azure security services and features](https://azure.microsoft.com/updates/?category=security).

## Additional resources

### Example solutions

- [Improved-security access to multitenant web apps from an on-premises network](../../web-apps/guides/networking/access-multitenant-web-app-from-on-premises.yml)
- [Restrict interservice communications](../../example-scenario/service-to-service/restrict-communications.yml)
- [Securely managed web applications](../../example-scenario/apps/fully-managed-secure-apps.yml)
- [Web app private connectivity to Azure SQL database](../../example-scenario/private-web-app/private-web-app.yml)

[Browse all our security architectures](/azure/architecture/browse/?azure_categories=security).

### AWS or Google Cloud professionals

- [Identity with Azure and AWS](../../aws-professional/security-identity.md)
- [AWS to Azure services comparison - Security](../../aws-professional/index.md#primary-topics)
- [Google Cloud to Azure services comparison - Security](../../gcp-professional/services.md#security-and-identity)

## Next steps

Security architecture is part of a comprehensive set of security guidance that also includes:

- [Security in the Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework for Azure](/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/secure): A high-level overview of a cloud security end state.
- [Azure Well-Architected Framework](/azure/architecture/framework/security/overview): Guidance on securing your workloads on Azure.
- [Azure security benchmarks](/security/benchmark/azure/): Prescriptive best practices and controls for Azure security.
- [End-to-end security in Azure](/azure/security/fundamentals/end-to-end): Documentation that introduces you to the security services in Azure.
- [Top 10 security best practices for Azure](/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/secure/security-top-10): Top Azure security best practices that Microsoft recommends based on lessons learned across customers and our own environments.
- [Microsoft Cybersecurity Architectures](/security/cybersecurity-reference-architecture/mcra): The diagrams describe how Microsoft security capabilities integrate with Microsoft platforms and 3rd-party platforms.

sections.blueprintInstructions: Write a brief introductory paragraph. Include why the category is important, the available Azure capabilities, and links to relevant technology choice guides.

questions.text: "These are some of the key security services" — the demonstrative "These" isn't followed by a noun, which violates the ensure_demonstratives_followed_by_noun rule. Should this be rewritten to "The following list describes some of the key security services available on Azure:" or similar phrasing?
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The ensure_demonstratives_followed_by_noun rule explicitly requires demonstratives to be followed by a noun for clarity. 'These' in 'These are' is not followed by a noun — it acts as a standalone pronoun.
questions.suggestion: Rewrite to: "The following list describes some of the key security services available on Azure:"
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: The suggested rewrite uses 'the following' with a noun ('list') and removes the bare demonstrative. Alternative rewrites such as 'These tools are some of the key security services' would also satisfy the rule.

questions.text: "conditional access" should be capitalized as "Conditional Access." Microsoft's terminology glossary explicitly lists "Conditional Access" as a proper feature name (capitalize_proper_nouns rule). The Microsoft Entra rename glossary confirms: "Azure AD Conditional Access" is now "Microsoft Entra Conditional Access (Second use: Conditional Access)."
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The Microsoft Entra terminology glossary at learn.microsoft.com/entra/fundamentals/new-name explicitly defines 'Conditional Access' as a capitalized proper feature name. The current text uses lowercase.
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: The fix is a direct capitalization correction backed by the official Microsoft Entra terminology glossary.

questions.text: "For a comprehensive description of Azure security tools and capabilities" — "comprehensive description" is verbose. The standard Microsoft Learn pattern is "For more information about." Consider rewriting to: "For more information about Azure security tools and capabilities, see End-to-end security in Azure." This aligns with the use_concise_sentence rule.
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The use_concise_sentence rule says to make every word count and remove words that don't add substance. 'Comprehensive description' uses two words where the simpler 'more information about' is the standard pattern. However, 'comprehensive description' is not egregiously verbose and does convey nuance that the target article is thorough.
questions.suggestion: Rewrite to: "For more information about Azure security tools and capabilities, see End-to-end security in Azure."
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: The suggested rewrite follows the standard Microsoft Learn cross-reference pattern. However, the original phrasing is not incorrect and 'comprehensive' does communicate that the target article offers broad coverage, so this is a moderate-confidence recommendation.

sections.blueprintInstructions:
Use this section to catalog the category's TOC entries.

Write this section in this exact order:

1. A short introductory paragraph for this H2.
2. `### {category} guides` if the TOC has a Guides node.
3. `### {category} architectures` if the TOC has an Architectures node.
4. `### {category} solution ideas` if the TOC has a Solution ideas node.

Core rules:

- The TOC YAML is the single source of truth for this entire section.
- Include only links that appear in the TOC.
- Do not invent links.
- Do not add links from the author's document unless they also appear in the TOC.
- Links in the author's document that are not in the TOC belong elsewhere, such as Best practices or Other resources, not here.
- If no TOC is available, use a narrow `STRUCTURAL` marker explaining that the repository TOC file for this category could not be located.
- Do not ask whether TOC-listed entries belong here. The TOC already decided that.
- Do not ask whether non-TOC links from the source document should be included here. They should not.

How to read the TOC:

- Skip the `Get started` node. That is this article.
- The `Guides` node maps to the guides subsection.
- The `Architectures` node maps to the architectures subsection.
- The `Solution ideas` node maps to the solution ideas subsection.
- Every leaf entry with an `href` becomes one bullet link.
- Build URLs as `/azure/architecture/{href}` and strip the `.yml` or `.md` extension.
- If a node contains sub-groups with their own `items`, render those groups as `####` headings.
- Include each link exactly once across the entire section.
- If a TOC node does not exist, omit that subsection. Do not add a marker for the missing subsection.

Use the author's document only for optional framing language in the short H2 introduction. Do not use the author's document to decide which entries belong in the subsections.

The introduction should briefly tell the reader that the section contains guides, production-ready architectures, and solution ideas for exploring {category} on Azure.

### {category} guides

Populate from the Guides node of the TOC. If no Guides node exists, omit this subsection.

Organization rules:

- Put technology choice guides first under the bold heading `**Technology choices:**`.
- Technology choice guides are choose-a-service and comparison articles.
- Put the remaining guides after that.
- If the TOC has sub-groups, use `####` headings for those groups.
- If the TOC does not naturally provide groupings beyond Technology choices, keep the subsection as a simple bulleted list.
- Do not invent guides, headings, or topic groups.

Formatting rules:

- Each entry is one bullet.
- Each bullet contains the link, a colon, and one short sentence of description.

Pattern:

**Technology choices:** The following articles help you evaluate and select the best {category} technologies for your workload requirements:

- [Guide name](/azure/architecture/...): Short description.

#### {TOC group name}

- [Guide name](/azure/architecture/...): Short description.

### {category} architectures

Populate from the Architectures node of the TOC. Include every entry, including entries nested inside sub-groups. If the TOC has sub-groups, use `####` headings, for example:

    #### Azure NetApp Files solutions
    - [Enterprise file shares with disaster recovery](/azure/architecture/example-scenario/file-storage/enterprise-file-shares-disaster-recovery): Deploy enterprise file shares with built-in disaster recovery using Azure NetApp Files.

The following production-ready architectures demonstrate end-to-end {category} solutions that you can deploy and customize:

Organization rules:

- Include every architecture entry from the TOC exactly once.
- Preserve TOC sub-groups when they exist.
- Do not invent extra topic layers when the TOC does not provide them.
- Use only the TOC entries. Do not add architecture links or thematic groupings from the source document unless those entries also appear in the TOC.
- Do not let source-document themes such as Identity, Threat protection, or other narrative categories change how the TOC entries are grouped or presented here.
- If the TOC does not group entries under a thematic heading, do not create one.
- Do not ask whether the source document's thematic groupings should influence this subsection. They should not.

Formatting rules:

- Prefer one sentence per entry.

Pattern:

### {category} solution ideas

The following solution ideas demonstrate implementation patterns and possibilities to explore:

- Treat this subsection as a lightweight catalog of TOC entries.
- Do not invent solution ideas.

- Use `####` headings only when the TOC provides sub-groups.

- If the TOC contains a nested group such as `Threat assessments`, keep that group as a `####` heading and list its child entries under it.
- Do not flatten nested TOC groups into a single list unless the TOC is already flat.
- Do not ask whether nested TOC grouping should be preserved. Preserve it.

questions.text: The bold lead-in terms (Security fundamentals:, Network security:, Data protection:, Threat protection:) use a colon between the term and definition. The Microsoft Style Guide term list format specifies: 'Use a period between the term and the definition.' Should the colon after each bold lead-in be changed to a period?
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The Microsoft Style Guide explicitly states 'Use a period between the term and the definition' in the Lists > Term lists section. The current draft uses colons, which directly contradicts this rule.
questions.suggestion: Change each bold lead-in from colon to period. For example: '**Security fundamentals.** These learning paths cover core security concepts and Azure security features:' — repeat for Network security, Data protection, and Threat protection.
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: The style guide rule is explicit and the fix is straightforward. The period-separated term list format is the documented standard.

questions.text: The topic area descriptions each end with a colon that introduces the bulleted list, but the bold term also uses a colon, creating a double-colon construction. If the term list period fix is applied, this resolves itself. No separate fix needed if the term-list period fix is applied.
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The colons guidance says to avoid using colons for clarification within sentences. Having two colons in one construction is atypical, but the second colon (before the list) follows the standard list-introduction pattern. The fix to the first colon resolves this.
questions.suggestion: Apply the term-list period fix from the previous marker. The second colon (before each bulleted list) is then the sole colon and follows standard list-introduction patterns.
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: This is a dependent fix — it resolves automatically if the term-list punctuation is corrected.

questions.text: The descriptions for Network security, Data protection, and Threat protection each begin with 'This learning path covers' (singular) while Security fundamentals uses 'These learning paths cover' (plural). The parallel structure is inconsistent. Should all topic areas use a consistent introductory pattern?
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The Microsoft Style Guide lists section states 'Make all the items in a list consistent in structure.' The singular/plural variation breaks parallel structure, though it accurately reflects the number of items in each group.
questions.suggestion: Use a consistent sentence pattern across all topic areas. Since Security fundamentals has multiple bullets and the others have one each, the singular/plural distinction is factually correct. Consider rephrasing to avoid the distinction, such as: 'Core security concepts and Azure security features:' or 'Learn about virtual network security, network segmentation, and secure connectivity:'
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: Multiple valid rewrites exist. The current wording is factually accurate (singular vs. plural matches the item count), so the parallel structure concern is moderate.

questions.text: The note says 'Check the certification page for the latest information.' The Microsoft Style Guide cross-reference pattern is typically 'For more information, see' or 'For the latest information, see.' Should 'Check' be changed to follow the standard cross-reference pattern?
questions.issueConfidenceReasoning: The style guide doesn't explicitly prohibit 'Check' as an imperative verb, but the standard Microsoft Learn cross-reference pattern uses 'see' rather than 'check.' This is a tone and convention issue rather than a hard rule.
questions.suggestion: Rewrite to: 'For the latest information, see the [certification page](/credentials/certifications/azure-security-engineer/).'
questions.suggestionConfidenceReasoning: The 'For more information, see' pattern is the standard convention on Microsoft Learn, but 'Check' is not explicitly prohibited. The suggestion aligns with the predominant pattern.
